issue.author: nikic

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
This is trickier than I thought. I think there are two complications here:

1. We can't simply read a larger value, because the read value might be refinement. For example, if we have `{ i32 0, i32 undef }`, and read a `i64` from that, then that i64 could legally be `i64 0`, but the memset would very much not be redundant.
2. Even if we have a `zeroinitializer`, I think we can still run into issues with padding. If we 8-byte memset a `{ i8, i32 } zeroinitializer`, then dropping the memset would leave the last 3 bytes in the middle uninitialized. However, those bytes might still be accessed (through a different type) and the lack of a memset would become visible.

Now, in practice, it's probably the case that any uninitialized part of a global effectively becomes zero-initialized, but that's probably not something we should rely on on the IR level.

comments.author: nikic
comments.body: Candidate patch: https://reviews.llvm.org/D128532 Doesn't cover the second point though.

issue.title: [GlobalOpt] Incorrect handling of memset in global ctor evaluation
